inclusive_assessment_strategies: Conversation
Product
Conversation: Can learners verbally analyse and understand the positive and negative impacts of agricultural changes on the environment, employment, and food security in the Caribbean?
Observation: Can learners appreciate how changes in the way food is produced and consumed in the Caribbean have had both positive and negative outcomes.
Observation: Can learners explore how shifts in agricultural practices, food imports, and dietary changes have affected the region’s economy, health, and environment.
Product: Are learners able to visually represent examples of forms of transportation?
Observation: Can learners suggest possible advantages and disadvantages of different forms of transportation?
Conversation: Can learners demonstrate how accessibility of transportation can help countries?
Conversation: Are learners able to define and give examples of forms of communication?
Conversation: Are learners able to explain the importance of regional communication among Caribbean countries?
Product: Are learners able to show the important events about each aspect of the transportation that brought Christopher Columbus to the Caribbean region during this early period?
Observation: Are learners able to sort pictures into a sequence to show the evolution and development of transportation and communication networks in the Caribbean region?

Conversation: Are learners able to evaluate the impact of technological advancements, such as the invention of the steam engine or the telegraph, on the evolution of transportation and communication networks in the Caribbean?
Conversation: Can learners define the terms transportation and communication?
Product: Can learners link goods and services to communication and transportation modes?
Conversation: Can learners explain how improvement to transport systems has made trading easier?
Product: Are learners able to highlight the economic benefits of using effective communication and transport systems for trade?
Product: Can learners determine positive and negative issues with trade?
Conversation: Are learners able to decide of the relative value of trade for the social and economic well-being of their country?
Conversation: Are learners able to list the main idea of the economic benefits of trading within the Caribbean region?
inclusive_learning_strategies:
The OECS IN 60 Seconds on Regional Integration
OECS Free Movement of Persons and Services
Invite learners to share what they have determined to be the main benefits of this regional integration. The following is another video about free movement across the region:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p3BvnIbPG-Q
Imagine this...
Share the following three scenarios with learners. Make sure they understand what visas, passports and work permits are.
We need a visa to go to each Caribbean nation
We need a work permit to work in all nations
We could only stay a short period on each island.
Invite learners to consider how these restrictions would contrast with the benefits identified in the previous two videos from the OECS. What benefits would be lost with these restrictions?

Hook/Engage the Learners:
Begin by asking learners about the types of food they eat and where these foods come from. Ask questions like:
"Do you know which Caribbean countries produce sugar, bananas, or coffee?"
"How do you think agriculture has changed since your grandparents’ time?"

Show a short photo slideshow depicting random images of historical and modern agriculture in the Caribbean (e.g., plantations, small farms, and modern technology in farming). This will spark curiosity and visual interest.
After inviting learners to put the pictures in order of time, use the pictures to identify the changes of agriculture over time.
Introduce Key Concepts:
What is Agriculture? (Provide a simple definition of agriculture: "Agriculture involves growing crops and raising animals to provide food and other products that people need")
Why is Agriculture Important? (Discuss how agriculture is important to Caribbean economies, providing jobs, food, and exports (e.g., sugar, bananas, coffee))
How Has Agriculture Changed? (Briefly explain that over the past century, many changes have occurred in the types of crops grown, farming methods used, and how agriculture is connected to other industries like tourism and manufacturing)
Agricultural Interviewing and Presentation
An elementary learner named Gracie Plair created the following YouTube video about Caribbean agriculture
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EY79zE1ICo0
She used Powtoon to develop the video:
http://www.powtoon.com/

Review this video with your learners and then ask them to engage with their local agricultural services office to explore the changes in agriculture in their island. Ask them to explore the following questions with their local agricultural office: 1) What do they know about the shift to small-scale farming from plantation farming after the emancipation of slaves and the rise of independent Caribbean nations? 2) When was there an introduction of new crops such as bananas, coffee, and cocoa that diversified the economy? 3) What has been the impact of natural disasters (hurricanes, volcanic eruptions) on agriculture, and how this led to more resilient farming methods? 4) Can they help explain how the global demand for Caribbean products (like sugar and bananas) fluctuated, causing changes in what farmers grew? 5) Can they discuss any trade agreements (e.g., with the U.S., Europe, other Caribbean Countries) and how these influence the rise and fall of certain crops in the region? 6) What technological advances in farming, such as mechanization, irrigation, and the use of fertilizers and pesticides have been recently introduced? 7) How has labour migration, especially to the U.S. and U.K., affected farming, with many Caribbean farmers leaving to seek work abroad? 8) Can they share how climate change has impacted Caribbean agriculture, has it led to more sustainable farming practices in recent decades?

9) What can they share about how organic farming and the push for more environmentally friendly agriculture has changed farming? Introduction to Agriculture in the Caribbean. The Teacher can share the following Calypso video about bananas in Dominica as way to pick out important agricultural themes: https://youtu.be/xBaTAThQwKA?feature=shared. W'ho Benefited from crop. W'hat issue with the industry. W'hat competition does it face. Invite learners to make their own calypso about the cause and effects of the changing nature of Caribbean agriculture since 1900.

Group Research Projects: Divide learners into groups and assign them specific crops (e.g., bananas, coffee, sugar) or islands (e.g., Jamaica, Dominica, Trinidad) to research. Each group will focus on how their assigned crop or island’s agriculture has changed since 1900. They can create digital presentations or posters which they will present to the class.

Role-Playing Activity: Set up a role-playing simulation where learners take on roles as different stakeholders in Caribbean agriculture (e.g., farmers, traders, government officials) and discuss the challenges and opportunities of farming in the 1900s versus today. This activity allows for hands-on learning, making it engaging for learners who prefer interactive experiences.

Hands-on Project: Farming Techniques Over Time (Practical Learners): Invite learners to work in groups to build models of farms from different time periods. For example, one group might create a model of a 1900s sugar plantation, while another creates a modern organic farm. Learners can compare and contrast the tools, labour force, and crops in each model, showing the evolution over time.

Step-by-Step Introduction to the Topic. Hook/Engage the Learners: Begin by asking thought-provoking questions such as: "How do you think farming affects the environment?" "What happens to jobs when new machines are introduced in farming?" "Does everyone have access to enough food in the Caribbean? Why or why not?"
The Teacher can be creative and set up the class like a radio interview, allowing learners to pretend they are callers giving their view.

Show a short video or documentary clip (e.g., Caricom Agriculture, National Geographic or a local Caribbean source) on modern farming practices, illustrating both the environmental and social effects. This will provide a visual introduction to the topic.
I Am Agriculture - John Jones
LIFE AS A FARMER LIVING IN JAMAICA!!!! HARVESTING PUMPKINS (Pt 717)
I Am Agriculture - Floyd Mathew
Introduce Key Concepts: - Use video and discussion method. Learners watch the video then have in class discussion

Environment: Explain how changes in agriculture affect natural ecosystems. For example, discuss deforestation for farming land, the use of chemicals (pesticides, fertilizers), and how modern practices can either harm or protect the environment.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=adHBSQw_gAc

Employment: Explore how agriculture provides jobs, but also how changes like mechanization (use of machines) can lead to job loss. Highlight both sides—while technology increases efficiency, it may reduce the need for labour.
TOP 10 CAREERS IN AGRICULTURE
Your Career in Agriculture

Food Security: Discuss how agricultural changes can increase food production and availability, but also how environmental damage or reliance on a single crop can lead to food shortages or reduced diversity in diets.
World Agricultural Expo: Using technology to increase production
Climate-Smart Agriculture: Helping the World Produce More Food
What is Sustainable Agriculture? Episode 1: A Whole-Farm Approach to Sustainability
Content to Include - Ask learners to create posters of the Positive and Negative Impacts. All posters must be presented verbally.

Positive Impacts: On the Environment: Introduction of sustainable farming practices (e.g., crop rotation, organic farming) reduces soil erosion, conserves water, and lowers the use of harmful chemicals. On Employment: Technology and modern farming techniques can increase productivity, leading to more food production and the creation of specialized jobs in the agricultural sector. On Food Security: Increased agricultural output can improve access to food, leading to lower prices and reducing hunger in many communities. The introduction of diverse crops can enhance nutrition and health.

Negative Impacts: On the Environment: Industrial agriculture can lead to deforestation, loss of biodiversity, and soil degradation. The excessive use of fertilizers and pesticides can pollute water sources and harm wildlife. On Employment: The mechanization of farming often leads to job losses for farm workers, particularly in rural areas. The shift from labour-intensive farming to machine-based farming can reduce employment opportunities. On Food Security: While modern agriculture increases food production, reliance on cash crops or monocultures (growing one type of crop) can reduce food diversity, leading to shortages if that crop fails due to climate changes or disease.
Radio Interview: The Teacher ends with the same radio interview of talk show activity with learners as callers.

Sample Questions for Radio Interview 1. Environmental Impact: o What are some positive ways modern farming can protect the environment? o How has agriculture damaged the environment in the past, and what solutions could help? 2. Employment Impact:
How has the introduction of machines in farming affected jobs in the Caribbean?
Can modern agriculture create new types of jobs in the region? If so, what kinds?
Food Security Impact: How can agricultural changes improve food availability for Caribbean people?
What are some risks to food security when farming becomes too dependent on a single crop?

Starting the Lesson: Engaging Learners Option 1: Field Trip Purpose: A field trip to a local farm, food processing plant, or market can provide a hands-on experience of modern agricultural practices and food production in the Caribbean. Field Trip Plan: Visit a local farm that produces crops or raises livestock using modern agricultural techniques. Have a tour guide explain the changes in farming practices, such as the introduction of fertilizers, pesticides, or mechanization, and how these have affected food production.
Visit a local market or supermarket to compare traditional and modern foods, discussing the availability of imported vs. locally produced items.
Invite learners to observe and interact with farmers, market vendors, or plant workers to hear their perspectives on the changes in food production over time

Post-Field Trip Activity: After the trip, learners can discuss what they learned, focusing on how these changes in food production have resulted in positive outcomes (e.g., increased food variety, economic growth) and negative outcomes (e.g., environmental degradation, loss of traditional farming methods). Learners can create posters or digital presentations highlighting both the benefits and drawbacks they observed.

Option 2: Movie or Documentary Screening Purpose: Watching a movie or documentary about food production and consumption in the Caribbean can provide a visual and engaging introduction to the topic. Suggested Films/Documentaries: "Island on the Edge" – Discusses how climate change is affecting food production and agricultural practices in small island nations like the Caribbean. Island on the edge

"Food Inc." – While focused on North America, this documentary highlights the global shift toward industrial food production and its effects, which can be compared to the Caribbean context. Food Inc - Documentary

Discussion Questions Post-Movie: "What positive changes have you seen in the Caribbean's food production?" "What challenges or negative outcomes are presented in the movie that could relate to the Caribbean?" "How can we balance the need for more efficient food production with the preservation of traditional practices and environmental sustainability?"

Hands-On Activity: Food Comparison (Kinaesthetic Learners) Organize a food tasting session where learners compare traditional Caribbean foods (e.g., fresh produce, ground provisions, locally grown fruits) with imported or processed foods. Have learners discuss how the introduction of imported goods and processed foods has changed the consumption patterns in the Caribbean and whether this is positive or negative for their health and culture.

Debate or Class Discussion (Communication Learners) Hold a class debate where one side argues that changes in food production have had mostly positive outcomes (e.g., economic growth, food variety) while the other side argues that the outcomes
Art: Create a picture collage of different forms or regional transportation in the Caribbean.
Group Inquiry: Divide learners into groups and assign them different perspectives (e.g., air, water, land) to discuss advantages and disadvantages of different forms of transportation
Advantages and Disadvantages of Each Mode of Transportation

Discussion: Discuss with the learners the importance of transportation accessibility for countries. Guide them to think about how transportation networks help in various aspects such as trade, tourism, access to resources, and cultural exchange. Encourage them to share examples of how transportation has benefited different countries in the Caribbean region.
Explain to learners that the following video has information about several forms of communication. They are asked to watch the video and choose their two favourite forms of communication.
Means of Communication video for kids | Communication video for kids
Give learners a simple a t chart and invite them to jot down their two forms of communication from the video on the top of the t chart.
Two forms

Details: Share some examples of the importance of regional communication on matters such as climate disasters, promotion of sports and entertainment, and marketing of goods and services. Invite learners to identify other examples of regional communication through radio, television or the internet. Invite learners to write down details of how they would communicate to people on other islands using these forms of communication.

Storytelling: Show one of the following YouTube videos about Christopher Columbus, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Eqn3X_ZPTd8 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A3DCiZas5Bo. Have learners Fold a piece of paper into three sections, write Start, Arrival, and Return in each section. After watching the video, have learners jot down a transportation event about Christopher Columbus in each block. Discuss the significance of this trip for subsequent travel in the sailing ship era.

Transportation and communication picture sort: Seek out the following pictures: Old style sailing vessel; steam powered train; Early steam ship; horse and wagon; hand cart; signal flag on hill; bonfire on hill; bus; small mini van; older style truck; newer style Kei truck; early letter sent by sailing ship; letter at post office; modern car, older style telephone, radio; television, mobile phone; computer; satellite; helicopter; modern passenger jet; turbo prop plan; canoe; small wooden vessel with sail; bicycle; electric bicycle. Learners will sort the following cards In small groups, Invite learners to sort these pictures from oldest to most recent. With learner assistance, place them on a timeline that dates from 1500 to the present in 50 year increments. Discuss with them the impact of each form of transportation and communication and help learners analyse pros and cons of each method
Class Review: Help learners review the previous lesson and define the terms communication and transportation.
ABC Product Brainstorm: Have learners fold a paper in half five times so that you end up with 28 squares

Jot down the alphabet with a letter on each square leaving the last two squares blank. Brainstorm one or two products they consume or use for each letter of the alphabet. Now, explore with them, through inquiry, where these products comes from. Are learners able to identify the origins within their own island, the region and beyond for various goods and services? Help learners see that this is called trade. On a second sheet and fold it into two columns, invite learners to pick a number of products or services that come from within the Caribbean region. Place these in column one. Use column two to jot down the mode of communication or transportation that is used to get them to their island.

Mapping Trade Routes: Use a map of the Caribbean and label the map and explain how goods and services from various islands are moved by sea and air and under sea cable and satellite. E.g. Trinidad oil, K. C trading confectioner, Digicel, plot major trade routes of LIAT, Caribbean airlines, coastal shipping via Caribbean shipping freight, and how transportation and communication have evolved over time. Divide learners into groups to research and present on different modes of transportation or communication in the Caribbean and their impact on trade. In groups have learners discuss: a) How communication has evolved over the years? b) How improvement in this technology has positively or negatively affected the way countries trade? c) Examine the factors which affect trade among countries?

Project- Based activity: Before proceeding with group work, assist learners to recall times when they have been at their island harbour when vessels arrived, or at the airport. Get them to describe the nature of the vessels and planes. Also, ask them if they know the location of satellite towers and if they cannot identify where satellite towers are located, describe this for them. Group project: Learners pick one form of transportation (sea, air) or communication (under sea cable, satellite) in the Caribbean. Ensure groups are diverse and balanced in terms of skills and learning styles. Have learners report on the ways that their transport system is an economic benefit for Caribbean countries. How improved communication has helped in the promotion of their island's economy in terms of trading? Learners are to provide specific examples of transportation and communication improvements in the Caribbean.

Data Disk Investigation: Whilst the Caribbean nations have many similarities, they also have many different types of natural resources, economic activities and artistes. Island nations benefit from the opportunity to access various resources, business products and services and skilled workers from each other. Form the class into six groups.

Provide each group with a data disk (circle with six pie slices). Place the following categories in each disk: special natural resources; unique foods; famous performing artists/musicians; well-known industries; and businesses. Choose one of the island nations of the Caribbean and identify some of these unique natural resources, foods, business products and services that the chosen country has. Once learners have completed their data disks, invite them to share. For example: Some islands do not have sources of gravel for roads but volcanic islands do. Some islands have better soil for growing certain types of foods. Some islands have famous musicians. Some islands have high tech industries or finance industries that require skilled workers In groups, learners display goods which are imported and exported. List the benefits of importing and exporting goods in their country. Share with learners the main features of the CARICOM Single Market and Economy, i.e. The establishment of a Single Market space which includes services, capital, technology, and the free movement of skilled professionals. What has this meant for the various peoples and nations of the Caribbean?

Plus-Minu-Interesting Chart Making: Learners can make a chart that identifies the pluses and minuses of trade between their country and others. What are the pluses? Identify all the positive aspects you can think of about trade with another country. E.g. Addresses food or shortage of other goods. What are the minuses? Identify all of the negative consequences of trade with other countries. E.g. Costs of transportation. What is interesting things about trade with other countries? These are points of interest about trade with other countries that you should pay attention to. E.g. Opportunity to receive goods that you would never have imagined on your own. Now after learners make their PMI charts engage learners in a discussion to make a balanced decision about the value (or not) to your country of trade with other countries. Increase capital due to greater exports

Finding and Appreciating the Main Idea: Have learners visit the OECS, CARICOIM, and Caribbean Development Bank websites. Listen to a speech of a Caribbean politician about some aspects of trade or economic development cooperation or read a policy about one of these features. Identify the main ideas that this speaker or document portrays that illustrate the benefits of economic cooperation and trade to the everyday lives of Caribbean people.
